observations.recommendation:
Require the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments to correct the double/erroneous recognition and classification of Land;

Direct both the Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer to reconcile their respective records against the Report of Physical Count of PPE before any adjustment is made to avoid double/erroneous recording of properties.

Direct the Municipal Health Officer:

Submit immediately to the Accounting Unit the copies of documents and other pertinent records of the donated ambulances to facilitate the recording in the books of accounts;

Secure documents such as DOD, PTR or any documents which transfer the ownership of the dental motorized chair unit with accessories and two Haier refrigerators to LGU Polanco, ZN to facilitate the recording in the books of accounts; and

We also recommended to require the department heads/end-users, whenever there is a property acquired through donation, to submit to the Accounting Unit the pertinent documents such as DOD, PTR or any documents which transfer the ownership of equipment to LGU Polanco, ZN.

We recommended to require the Municipal Accountant to recognize assets and payables in the books of accounts based on actual deliveries and services rendered supported with appropriate documents. Moreover, the Supply Officer is to accept only those complete deliveries. In case of partial deliveries, require the supplier to show in the DR only those items actually delivered.

Supply Officer to prioritize the finalization of IIRUP, including the identification of methods of disposal for the above unserviceable equipment and the conduct of disposal, pursuant to COA Circular 92-386 to avoid further deterioration of these equipment which would eventually affects the saleable value, and submit the approved IIRUP and other documents to the Municipal Accountant; and

Municipal Accountant is to derecognize the disposed PPEs from the books, upon receipt of the IIRUP and other documents from the Supply Officer.

11. We recommended to require the Supply Officer to furnish the Municipal Accountant copies of AIR and DR after inspection and delivery, and prepare SSMI to consolidate all RIS and submit them to the Municipal Accountant weekly. Also, based on said documents, the Municipal Accountant is to record inventory transactions in the books of accounts and recognized in the financial statements of the periods to which they relate.

27. We recommended that the Committee on Family, Women, Children, and Elderly of the SB endorse the passing of an ordinance localizing RA No. 11313 and post the same in conspicuous places. Further, we recommend that the ordinance include provisions on the following: a) measures to prevent gender-based sexual harassment in educational institutions; b) imposition of fines/penalty for violation thereof; and c) creation of sexual harassment hotlines.

29. We recommended that the Committee on Women and Family with the assistance of the GAD Focal Point System comply with the revisions required by the SP for the approval of the proposed ordinance of GAD Code for the LGU and submit the same to the SP for enactment into Ordinance to fully support the LGU’s efforts in promoting, protecting and fulfilling women’s human rights, women’s economic empowerment and gender-responsive governance for the attainment of gender equality and women’s empowerment.

We also recommended that the Inventory Committee sign/certify the correctness
of the RPCPPE report to be approved by the Local Chief Executive, and submit to the
Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that:

a) Management constitute a Disposal Committee in accordance with the COA-DBM
Joint Circular No. 2024-001 and facilitate the immediate disposal of these
unserviceable properties;

b) Management assess at each reporting date whether there is any indication that
the unserviceable PPE items may be impaired. If any such indication exists, the
recoverable service amount of these assets shall be estimated; and

c) The Municipal Accountant recognize Impairment Loss and provide Allowance for
Impairment for unserviceable properties at year-end, with corresponding disclosure in
the Notes to FS of the amount and details of impairment losses recognized during
the period to fairly present the proper valuation of the affected PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact with the AGDB the
balance of the Cash – Local Treasury account as of year-end, in accordance with Section
32 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I, to safeguard the funds from risks of
loss or misuse.

We recommended that management:

a. Demand from the concerned accountable officers the immediate liquidation of
the cash advances; and

b. Closely monitor the granting and liquidation of cash advances to ensure
compliance with the aforecited provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002

We also recommend that Management regularly review its accomplishments to ensure
that all potential revenue sources are fully utilized, in line with its legally mandated
revenue-generating authority.

We recommended that Management:

a) Submit an explanation/justification for the engagement of the services of a lawyer as
a consultant on legal matters without securing written concurrence from the
Commission on Audit;

b) Submit the documents required in filing for COA’s written concurrence in the
hiring of a private lawyer per the 2024 COA’s Citizens Charter to the Audit Team; and

c) Henceforth, comply with the requirement of COA’s written concurrence in the hiring
of a private lawyer, law firm, or legal consultant in accordance with COA Circular
No. 98-002, as well as the documentary requirements per COA Circular No. 2012-
001.

We recommended that Management:

a) Submit an explanation for the noted deficiencies in the procurement of a
Highly Technical Consultant and the documents required in accordance with the 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and

b) Refund the proportionate amount unearned for March 2024.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management improve its
performance in the submission of financial reports required in Annex S of the PRDP
Financial Management Operations Guidelines within the prescribed periods.

We recommended that Management:

a) Submit the document authorizing the claimant of the check and a copy of a valid ID
as a means of identification as supporting documents to the DV;

b) Explain why there is a discrepancy in the name of the contractor per the Affidavit
vs. other documents submitted;

c) Ensure that in the succeeding payments, the dates and information such as Check
No., are properly reflected in the spaces provided for in the DV. Also, see to it that
supporting documents such as Concrete Production Records contain the necessary
details for proper evaluation; and

d) Through the Municipal Treasurer, stamp as “Paid” the DVs and all their supporting
documents after payment to prevent their possible re-use.

We recommended that henceforth,

a. The Municipal Accountant provide adequate, necessary, and relevant disclosures in
the Notes to FS as to the actual status of implementation of the PRDP subproject,
including information on the extension of its project completion, to enhance the fair
presentation and usefulness of its financial statements; and

b. The Municipal Engineer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with relevant
documents such as Statement of Work Accomplished, extension of project
completion, and other information on the status of implementation of the PRDP
subproject as of year-end, for disclosure in the Notes to FS.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive
direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the Monthly Trial Balance and
the Quarterly and Year-end Financial Statements within the prescribed timeframe in
accordance with existing rules and regulations.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive
direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the Monthly Trial Balance and
the Quarterly and Year-end Financial Statements within the prescribed timeframe in
accordance with existing rules and regulations.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant
prepare and submit the BRS within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with
existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the LSB ensure that annual and quarterly reports on SEF
Utilization prepared by the Municipal Accountant are submitted to DepEd CO, a copy
furnished to the Regional Office of the DBM within the deadline set forth in item 6.1 of
the Revised Guidelines on the Use of the Special Education Fund.

We commended the Management for implementing and completing most of its
targeted PPAs funded under the 20 per cent Development Fund, attaining its desired
socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the
promotion of the general welfare of the public.

Further, we recommended that Management enjoin the collective efforts of all
concerned departments to study its unimplemented PPAs so that strategies may be
determined and its implementation could run smoothly and efficiently.

We recommended that Management improve its performance in the submission of
the GAD Accomplishment Report in compliance with the deadline prescribed under JMC
No. 2022-01.

We reiterated our recommendation that the focal point/person for the GAD
Program of the agency adhere strictly to the proper recognition of Gender-Related
Issues in considering programs, projects, and activities for the GAD Plan and Budget in
the succeeding year.

We likewise reiterated our recommendation that the members of the GAD Focal
Point System attend seminars on HGDG to enhance compliance with the proper
preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget, and Accomplishment Report.

We recommended that Management adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5
of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA
Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end
of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and
the Municipal Development Council.

We commended the Management for maximizing the utilization of the LDRRMF
to cover all the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management to
enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen, or limit
their adverse impacts on the community.

In addition, we recommended that the Management continue its commendable
performance in maximizing the utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Fund (LDRRMF).

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the remittance of the
unremitted balances due to the BIR to avoid penalties and/or surcharges that may be
imposed for the non/delayed remittance of taxes.

We commended the Management for facilitating the timely remittance of
contributions to the GSIS/PhilHealth/HDMF.

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a) Review and reconcile the composition of the amounts Due to GSIS/Pag-IBIG
accounts and facilitate the immediate remittance to the GSIS and HDMF of the
amounts withheld from LGU personnel/ or refund to the concerned LGU employees, as
appropriate;

b) Disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the composition and reasons for
non-remittance of the Due to GSIS/PhilHealth/Pag-IBIG accounts, if applicable.

We recommended that:

1) the Municipal Accountant promptly adjust the reconciling items in the books to
present the correct balance of Cash in Bank – Local Currency account in the
financial statements;

2) the Municipal Treasurer transmit all relevant accounts, including Manager’s
Checks and corresponding deposit slips, to the Municipal Accountant to serve as
basis for recording the reconciling items and the transferred balances from closed
accounts; and

3) the Municipal Treasurer initiate the preparation of a letter addressed to LBP to
inquire the status of the closure of accounts.

9. We recommended to direct the Inventory Committee to expedite the preparation of revised RPCPPE for one-time cleansing and submit to COA. Also, require the Property and Accounting Units to establish the non-existing/missing PPEs and follow the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to complete the conduct of one-time cleansing.

10. We recommended to require the Municipal Accountant to properly include and present the necessary disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statement as provided in par.122 of IPSAS 1 and in accordance with the format provided under COA Circulars No. 2015-008 and 2016-004 to ensure that the end users are provided with complete information and reliable financial statements.

12. We recommended to direct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to adjust their respective records for the unappropriated surplus as of December 31, 2023 by excluding the amount of P364,000.00 so that only those backed-up with existing funds are thereafter be available for subsequent reappropriation. Also, ensure that savings identified are accurate to avoid passing of an Appropriation Ordinance/Supplemental Budget and disbursements of the same without actual available funds.

13. We recommended to expedite in identifying available funds to be subjected for supplemental budget to cover up the cash overdraft/deficit totaling P90,269.67. Also, require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that funds are used solely for their intended purpose and refrain from disbursing beyond the allotted funds for the corresponding PPAs. The management is encouraged to regularly monitor the Municipality’s cash position to avoid the same incurrence in its future operations.

14. Direct the Implementing Office to obtain approval from the source agency for the utilization of said funds for another PPA. Otherwise, require the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the return of the unused funds of the completed PPAs to the source agency, in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013; and

15.The Municipal Agriculturist to facilitate the preparation of certification as required by DOST to expedite the revision of MOA for the immediate commencement of the project implementation.

19. Municipal Treasurer to immediately prepare a check to transfer the unexpended and unobligated balance of DRRMF for CY 2023 from the GF to the TF and the Municipal Accountant to recognize the transfer and receipt of DRRMF in the GF and TF books, respectively; and

20. Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to subsequently transfer the amount of unexpended and unobligated current balance of the LDDRMF to the TF at the end of the year and not merely as set-up obligation in the GF books.

26. We recommended to direct the Municipal Development Council, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, and Municipal Engineer to ensure that identification of projects to be funded out of the 20 per cent DF are well planned, and procurement-and-implementation ready to avoid implementation issues such as right of way acquisition, site problem and other issues in order that at least 75 per cent of the 20 per cent DF are optimally utilized. Also, require the implementing Office to submit PRs timely and in accordance with the projected procurement activity to ensure that PPAs are completed during the year.
28. We recommended to create and establish Monitoring and Evaluation Team in consonance with the provision Section 5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to evaluate the LGU GAD PPAs.

34. Management to inform our Office within 10 days after the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted in order for our Office to validate the same.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

Require the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing law, rules and regulations;

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We reiterate our previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the Monthly Trial Balance and the Quarterly and Year-end Financial Statements within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Property and Accounting Units undertake the necessary reconciliation procedures as outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the correct balance of the PPE and record the necessary adjustments.  

Further, that the format for RPCPPE prescribed by MNGAS, Volume II, be used and a summary per type of PPE be prepared thereof for reconciliation purposes with the Accounting records and that the same shall be certified correct by all members of the Inventory Committee.

Submit through electronic mail a copy of its APP and APCPI results to the GPPB, in both Microsoft Excel and PDF File; and

Post the APP and PMR in the agency website or in a conspicuous place reserved for this purpose in the premises of the Municipality and submit a certification indicating that it has complied with either of the said posting requirements to the GPPB.

We recommended that:

the Municipal Accountant promptly adjust the reconciling items in the books to present the correct balance of Cash in Bank – Local Currency account in the financial statements;

the Municipal Treasurer transmit all relevant accounts, including Manager’s Checks and corresponding deposit slips, to the Municipal Accountant to serve as basis for recording the reconciling items and the transferred balances from closed accounts; and

the Municipal Treasurer initiate the preparation of a letter addressed to LBP to inquire the status of the closure of accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Property and Accounting Units undertake the necessary reconciliation procedures as outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the correct balance of the PPE and record the necessary adjustments.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant include the necessary disclosures on the total road network system of the LGU in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We commended Management for preparing the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP), submitted to the Audit Team on May 9, 2024. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 89-296.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% Local Development Fund programs and projects to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the general public.

We commended Management for appropriately recording in the books of accounts the transfer to the Special Trust Fund of the unexpended balance of the 5% LDRRMF for CY 2023.

Furthermore, we recommended that Management ensure the transfer of actual cash to Special Trust Fund pertaining to unexpended balance of current DRRMF at year end.

We recommended that Management adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.

Further, we recommended that Management submit the other Utilization Report thereof for validation purposes.

We commended the efforts exerted by the Local Chief Executive in coordination with the LDRRMO and other stakeholders in the improvement of the utilization rate of the mitigation fund to 80.30 per cent.

We also recommended that Management continue to improve and maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF to cover all four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management in order to enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen or limit their adverse impacts to the community.

We therefore recommended that the focal point/person for the GAD Program of the agency to adhere strictly with proper recognition of Gender-Related Issues in considering programs ‘projects/and activities for the GAD Plan and Budget in the succeeding year.

We likewise reiterated our recommendation that the members of the GAD Focal Point System attend seminars on HGDG to enhance compliance with the proper preparation of the GAD PB/AR.

We commended the Management’s compliance in creating a Solid Waste Management Board in accordance with Section 12 of R.A. No. 9003 and Section 5, Rule VI of DENR Administrative Order No. 2001-34 or the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9003.
We commended the Management and the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board for reviewing and updating the Solid Waste Management Plan every year pursuant to Section 16 of RA 9003.

We commended the Municipality and the Local School Board for providing for equitable budget for all the priority programs, projects and activities allowable as SEF expenditures and maximizing the utilization thereof, addressing the resource gap in DepEd’s budget for the operation and maintenance of public schools under the LGU’s jurisdiction.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all concerned officials and employees to adequately plan all activities related to project implementation. This includes preparing a project implementation plan in line with the terms and conditions of both the Implementation Management Agreement with the DA and the Contract with the supplier to facilitate proper monitoring of the I-BUILD sub-project and ensuring that the target percentages of completion are met to achieve timely delivery of optimal benefits to the project beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit the BRS together with the bank statements and attachments within the prescribed period.

We recommended the following courses of action:

The Municipal Assessor shall furnish the Municipal Treasurer the updated Assessment Roll (AR) as early as possible in order for the latter to update her Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR) as basis for the preparation of the Certified List.

The Municipal Treasurer shall furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list extracted from her RPTAR showing names of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to support the RPT/SET receivables recorded at the beginning of the year and to improve the collection of taxes.  This is also useful in monitoring arrearages as well as benchmark in determining collection efficiency.

The Municipal Accountant shall establish the Real Property/Special Education Tax Receivable accounts based on the said certified lists at the beginning of the year drawing a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the debit to Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and crediting the Deferred Real Property Tax Income and Deferred Special Education Tax Income as required under Section 20 of NGAS, Vol. I.

Likewise, the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to reconcile their records with the Municipal Assessor to come up with an accurate real property tax receivable.

We commended the Management in ensuring efficient implementation and substantial utilization of the LDRRMF on planned Programs, Projects and Activities (PPAs) to promote a more disaster resilient community and help reduce disaster risks.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% Local Development Fund programs, projects and activities to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the general public.

We recommended that, henceforth, the Municipality issue the NOA within 10 calendar days from receipt of the No Objection Letter 2 (NOL2), as required in Section B.30 of the PRDP Module 1: Guidelines for the Procurement of Infrastructure Subprojects under PRDP Second Additional Financing with EU Grant co-financing.

We recommended that, henceforth, the Municipality enter into contract within 15 calendar days from receipt by the winning bidder of the NOA, as required under Section B.30 of the PRDP Module 1: Guidelines for the Procurement of Infrastructure Subprojects under PRDP Second Additional Financing with EU Grant co-financing.

We recommended that Management submit henceforth, copies of all perfected government contracts and all supporting documents within five working days from execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 to facilitate timely auditorial and technical review thereof.

We recommended that Management, henceforth, prepare one check per DV per fund source pursuant to Item a(3) of Section 4.2.4.3 of the FMOG of the PRDP under the DA to facilitate monitoring of expenditures per fund source of the project and to maintain an effective internal control on disbursements.
We recommended that Management submit, henceforth, the financial reports required in Annex S of the PRDP Financial Management Operations Guidelines within the prescribed periods.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the BRS within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with existing rules and regulations, together with the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) for recording of the adjustments/reconciling items, for review and verification.

We recommended that Management direct the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Audit Team the CRAAF for July, August and December 2022.

Likewise, we reiterate our recommendation to ensure the timely submission and faithful compliance of the monthly the CRAAF in order to prevent possible loss and misuse of accountable forms.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) henceforth to direct the Municipal Health Office to report all COVID-19 related cases to DOH for transparency and monitoring purposes.

We recommended that Management submit a copy of duly submitted reports to DOH that contains all COVID related patients handled by the Rural Health Unit (RHU) or quarantine facility, if any.
We recommended that the Management prepare the SRA payroll in accordance with Section 2.e of AO No. 36 and refund the said amount due to improper computation.

We recommended that Management provide a TOR, or in the contract with each consultant the specific nature of work assignments and/or specific duties and responsibilities, quantity of work to be accomplished and the desired work outputs as well as the reporting requirements and working time to facilitate comparison and evaluation of actual work accomplishments of hired consultants.
We recommended that the Municipality refrain from entering into contract prior to the issuance of BAC Resolution, as BAC Resolution form as an integral part of the contract.
We recommended that the Municipality refrain from initiation of contracts prior to the issuance of NTP in compliance with Section 37.4.1 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing observation, we recommended to Management the following:

Direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash-in Local Treasury account with the pertinent Cashbooks and make the necessary adjustments thereon; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the reconciling items in the books of accounts based on the BRS prepared. Moreover, furnish the Municipal Treasurer copies of the BRS for the recording of reconciling items, if any, pertaining to its records.
We recommended that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer maintain Cashbook-Cash in Bank by fund and by account number to facilitate reconciliation of the treasury and accounting records.
We recommended that the agency determine the accountabilities of AOs based on the average monthly disbursement in a year to ensure that the AOs are sufficiently covered with bond.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a request for authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts amounting to ₱1,099,345.54 following the procedures in Sections 8.2 and 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management submit the above-mentioned documentary requirements in support of the payment for terminal leave benefits.

We recommended that henceforth Management support all transactions with valid, complete and proper documents as required under Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

In line with the foregoing observation, we recommended to Management the following:

Require the end-user/implementing units to prepare the PPMPs in accordance with Section 7.1 of the RIRR of RA 9184 and make sure that only those considered crucial to the efficient discharge of government functions shall be included; and

Require the Municipal Budget Office to thoroughly evaluate the submitted PPMPs in coherence with the proposed budget.

We recommended that the LCE henceforth direct the BAC to utilize the GPPB approved format of the APP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the agency prepare its APCPI and immediately submit a copy to GPPB TSO. Whereas, APCPI results are among the required documents to be submitted to GPPB for the grant of Performance-Based Bonus every year starting CY 2015.
We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to keep abreast of the latest promulgation of the GPPB regarding the standard forms need to be submitted by the prospective bidders.

We recommended that the agency conduct the pre-bid conference twelve (12) calendar days before the deadline for the submission and receipt of bids, but not earlier than seven (7) calendar days from the PhilGEPS posting of the Invitation to Bid (ITB) or bidding documents in accordance with Section 22.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipality impose liquidated damages corresponding to the number of days delayed pursuant to Section 68, Rule XXII of the RIRR of RA 9184, if no justifiable reason was communicated by the supplier and approved by the LCE.

We recommended that Management:

Submit the lacking required documents; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure submission of complete required documents in the procurement of goods.

We recommended that the agency refrain from procuring items/goods which are not included in the approved APP including approved changes thereto in compliance with Section 7.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the submission of copies of contracts and supporting documents to the Audit Team within five days after perfection thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to invite at least two observers from a duly recognized private group in a sector or discipline relevant to the procurement at hand and from an NGO in every stage of procurement to enhance transparency of procurement process.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to ensure compliance and completeness of the documentary requirements as prescribed by the aforementioned laws and regulations and for the Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents before payment.

Municipal Mayor, through the MSWDO, revisit its existing guidelines and policies on the grant of financial assistance to its constituents to (i) incorporate possible update in the allowable amount being granted, (ii) set payment threshold to funeral parlor, transport company, accredited drugstore or hospital, as the case may be, and (iii) include additional guidelines deemed necessary to promote more equitable assistance that is the same for similar conditions. Otherwise, comply with the exiting internal guidelines to facilitate the granting of assistance; b. Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials provide justification on the granting of financial assistance to other local government units after the occurrence of the festival celebration and henceforth, refrain from granting similar assistance to other LGUs that no longer need such; and c. Municipal Accountant submit the lacking disbursement vouchers and supporting documents for the payment of burial expenses to funeral service providers for validation of the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: DVs and supporting documents pertaining to burial assistance amounting ₱201,679.68 were submitted on June 18, 2021. Copy of the guidelines on the grant of financial assistance was submitted.
observations.reason: No response was submitted for the grant of financial assistance to other local government units after the occurrence of the festival celebration, precluding the audit of the transactions. On the other hand, the disbursement vouchers for the payment of burial expenses to Funeral Service Providers totaling ₱201,679.68 were submitted for audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and/or Disbursing Officers: a. strictly monitor officers and employees with outstanding cash advances and refrain from granting additional cash advances to an employee prior to settlement of his/her previous cash advances; b. refrain from granting cash advance which exceeds the maximum cash accountability covered by the bond of the Accountable Officer, otherwise, consider increasing the bond of the DO; c. consider designating other bonded employees as Special Disbursing Officers to cash advance for special purpose/undertaking, whenever necessary, to avoid overlapping cash advances and to minimize the risk of non-collection of the amount granted to disbursing officers exceeds their bond coverage; and d. ensure that liquidation of the accountable officers are within the reglementary period in compliance with the prescribed guidelines under COA Circular Nos. 97-002, 2012-001 and 2012-004, as applicable. We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to submit the liquidation documents of cash advances totaling ₱527,060.00 for validation and audit purposes.
observations.managementAction: Copy of liquidation report were submitted on June 18, 2021.
observations.reason: Cash advances were still granted despite having previous CAs not yet fully liquidated. Moreover, some CAs were also liquidated beyond prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the MSWMB, through the MENRO Designate, coordinate with the DENR-EMB to secure approval of its SWM Plan as early as possible in order to effectively implement SWM programs. Moreover, assess the funding and resource requirements to ensure implementation and realization of all SWM programs and projects on time.
observations.managementAction: The 10-year SWM Plan was prepared and submitted. The Management is still waiting for the schedule of defense to secure approval of its plan. The MENRO submitted revised 10-year plan.
observations.reason: SWM programs not yet implemented due to unavailability of lot for the establishment of sanitary landfill. Moreover, the programmed PPAs for SWM implementation was not updated and suitable for the current needs of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the: a. Local Development Council, Implementing Office and MPDC, in the planning stage, (i) indicate the period of implementation of the PPAs identified in the AIP considering various factors such as urgency of the project and the climate or weather factor; (ii) adopt a mode of implementation that will fast track the completion of their programmed developmental projects; and (iii) evaluate the viability of the projects as well as the need for any additional fund needed to complete a project programmed in the previous year; b. Municipal Engineer formulate strategic plan that will aid in the effective and efficient coordination, monitoring, reporting and evaluation of the status of implementation of the PPAs for the optimal utilization of 20% DF; and c. The Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant review and analyze the unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects and those already identified as non-priority projects as well as those already funded, for reversion to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 322 of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: Programs/ Projects and Activities were implemented by Management. Some of the Municipality’s PPEs were insured.
observations.reason: Utilization of fund was still not optimally utilized during the year. Some of the high value properties were still not insured.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor consider insuring all of the Municipality’s insurable assets especially those high value properties with the GIF of the GSIS and include the required premium in its annual budget. As such, we recommend that the Inventory Committee, Municipal Engineer and GSO-Designate (i) exert extensive effort in accomplishing the RPCPPE, and PPE-related reports to be used in application of insurance of properties and (ii) prepare the Property Inventory Form listing all insurable assets, properties and interests of the Municipality following the guidelines and format prescribed in COA Circular 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018, and submit the same to GSIS, copy furnished the Audit Team, on or before April 30 of each year as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality's PPEs were insured.
observations.reason: Some of the high value properties were still not insured.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and other offices concerned: a. ensure that PPAs included in the DILG-endorsed GPB were integrated in the AIP and provided with adequate resources of at least five percent (5%) of the Annual Budget; b. inform in writing the DILG concerned, through the Municipal Mayor, of any changes in the DILG-endorsed GPB or if there is a need to implement additional PPAs relevant to current gender issues or GAD related undertakings which will be properly reflected in the GAD AR pursuant to Annex E of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01; c. facilitate the implementation of the PPAs included in the DILG-endorsed GPB and refrain from utilizing funds other than for which they have been appropriated; d. consistently observe the guide in completing the GAD Accomplishment Report prescribed under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01 especially in accomplishing Columns 12, 13 and 14; e. exhaust all available resources for the collection and processing of complete and reliable data requirements of the GAD Database to be used in data processing and gender analysis for gender-responsive planning and budgeting; and f. make representation with the Sangguniang Bayan to consider amending its existing GAD Code to include provision for the development of IRR indicating among others the groups and entities which shall promulgate the same.
observations.managementAction: The Management attributed a total of ₱20.25 million or equivalent to 16.08% of the Municipal budget as GAD expenditures. The 10-year SWM plan was prepared and submitted. The Management is still waiting for the schedule of defense to secure approval of its plan.
observations.reason: Attributed PPAs in accomplishment report were not the same PPAs in the DILG-endorsed plan. GAD database were still incomplete and other information were not properly and comprehensively presented and sex-disaggregated. Absence of IRR for the GAD Code.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: Municipal’s SWM Board in coordination with the Municipal Health Officer and other agencies concerned prioritize the schedule for the necessary training for the SWM staff to ensure that the solid wastes are handled properly to minimize the risk of hazards to the SWM personnel and the public as well; Designated MENRO through the help of the OIC-Municipal Budget Officer provide appropriation for the procurement of the personal protective equipment and cleaning materials, coordinate with the procurement officers for the purchase of the required items and eventually issue the same to the SWM staff for them to properly and safely carry out their tasks; and Municipal’s SWM Board seek for additional fund which will be used in the implementation of the proper collection, transportation and handling of solid wastes required by the provisions of the IRR of RA No. 9003.

observations.reason: No allotted budget for the training relative the solid waste collection, transportation, handling, usage and maintenance of equipment & even prevention on the damage to the container and spillage of waste within the collection vicinity.

(iv) issuance of demand letters to stallholders to encourage the timely

coordinate to enforce collection of rental fees and corresponding

Statement for fair presentation.
We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang
Bayan agreed to take equitable and legal measures to address the issue of
informal settlers situated in the public market site to give way to maximization
of its intended purpose and development.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and MDRRMO
through the help of the Municipal Engineer (a) submit all the lacking

c. Procurement of one unit of laptop out of DRRM Fund
Verification of the transactions paid out of DRRM Fund revealed that one unit of
laptop costing ₱38,000.00 was procured out of the continuing appropriations of
DRRM Fund covered by Disbursement Voucher (DV) No. 100-2017-10-77 under
Check No. 520320 dated October 23, 2017.
Scrutiny of the supporting documents disclosed the following:
1. Purchase Order (PO) was incompletely filled-up as to delivery period, place of
delivery, payment term and mode of procurement. The same also doesn’t bear
the signature of the Municipal Accountant as to the availability of funds. PO
number and date were handwritten which casted doubt as to validity if the
document. No date of conforme by the Quadroman Trading was also indicated
in the PO.
2. The laptop was received by the Assistant MDRRMO and issued by the OIC-
GSO on October 2, 2017, however, PO which served as the contract
agreement was only dated October 9, 2017. Moreover, the delivery of the
equipment took place on October 10, 2017 as evidenced by the Sales Invoice
(SI) No. 1067 issued by the supplier and subsequently inspected and accepted
by the Inspection and Property Officers on October 12, 2017 as indicated in
the Inspection and Acceptance Report (IAR), which casted doubt as to
whether the equipment was received even before PO was created.
3. No purpose was indicated in the Purchase Request (PR) relative the
acquisition of one unit of laptop.
Item 5.5 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1
dated March 25, 2013 states the examples of the disaster equipment to be used for
the disaster response and rescue activities as well as the expendable items.
Moreover, DILG Memorandum Circualr No. 2012-73 dated April 17, 2012 list
down the early warning systems, preparedness equipment and other equippage
which may be procured out of the 70% of the 5% LDRRMF. Unfortunately,
laptop was not included among the list, and the Team believes that procurement
of the same should be out of the Capital Outlay of the MDRRM Office and not
45
out of DRRM fund of the Municipality since the same will be used greatly on the
discharge of their administrative functions.
We recommended that the (a) MDRRMO submit satisfactory
explanation relative the procurement of one unit of laptop out of DRRM
Fund; (b) concerned officials and employees justify for the obsevations noted
in the procurement process and (c) MDRRMO seek the assistance of the
OIC-Budget Officer for the appropriate sourcing of fund for the MDRRM
Office relative the discharge of their administrative functions.
Management comment
In reply to the AOM issued, the MDRRMO justified that the purpose of
purchasing one unit of laptop is mainly for the use of disaster risk reduction
through the continuous information and educational campaign and trainings with
the barangays. According to them, without the laptop the DRRM office cannot
effectively deliver the information to the general public when presenting the IEC
presentations.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
No comment was received relative the noted observations in the
procurement process and in the conflict of dates in the receipt by the Asst.
MDRRMO of the laptop and the dates of PO, delivery and IAR.
d. Inefficient management of inventory of disaster equipment, supplies and
materials and spoilage of canned goods for stockpile
The MDRRMO prepared Rescue vehicles, Equipment and Accessories Inventory
as of December 2017 for all the procured disaster equipment, supplies and
materials which were in the custody of MDRRMO as of December 31, 2017.
However, conduct of ocular inspection on the reported inventory revealed the
following:
 The disaster equipment, supplies and materials were kept in the MDRRM
Office and in the Municipal Auditorium and not organized as seen in the
pictures taken during the conduct of inspection on March 19, 2018.
Inspection of DRRM supplies and materials at the MDRRM Office
Inspection of rescue vehicles and generator set within the vicinity of the MDRRM Office
Inspection of DRRM supplies and materials at the Municipal Auditorium
During ocular inspection, it was observed that the MDRRM Operation Center was
already constructed and ready for occupancy after minor finishing. It is proposed
to house the DRRM equipment, supplies and materials. Pictures below show the
present MDRRM Office and the newly-constructed MDRRM Operation Center.
Inspection of present MDRRM Office
Inspection of newly-constructed MDRRM Operation Center
48
 The inventory was incomplete as to some acquisition dates and data such as
description, color and serial numbers and sizes, if applicable. The acquisition cost
for the items was also not indicated. The following items remarked as
unserviceable and found to be unserviceable during ocular inspection but still
included in the inventory:
Description Acquisition Qty. Location Remarks
Date
Speed boat,150hp 2008 1 Kabilang Ibayo unserviceable
Rubber boat 2014 1 Mun. Auditorium unserviceable
Motorola, handheld radio 2014 1 MDRRM Office unserviceable*
Chainsaw, Stihl small 2014 Mun. Auditorium unserviceable
Tent 2011 1 Mun. Auditorium unserviceable*
Folding bed, blue 2011 3 Mun. Auditorium unserviceable
Rice cooker 2015 1 MDRRM Office unserviceable*
Legend: *-found to be defective during ocular inspection
Those defective and unserviceable items should be listed in the Waste Materials
Report and the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable
Property(IIRUP) for possible disposal in accordance with the applicable rules and
regulations on supply and property management. There were also inconsistencies
in the location of the equipment, supplies and inventory, since the report stated
that the item was located in MDRRM Office, however, upon ocular inspection,
the item was found in Municipal Auditorium or vice versa.
 There were items included in the inventory such as helmet, rescue vest, GPS and
rescue knives which were indicated as located in the MDRRM Office, however,
during the inspection were found to be issued to rescuers. It was also observed
that there were no property numbers attached in various DRRM equipment.
 It was also noted that the rescue motorcycle and generator set found during the
inspection, however, not included in the inventory. Some of the items were not
properly labeled thus resulting in the difficulty of conducting inventory-taking.
It was also noted that 17 boxes of canned goods (Mega sardines) were
found in the small stockroom at the back of the stage in the Municipal plaza. It
was observed that these canned goods were already spoiled and cannot be used
anymore for stockpiling and relief operations. However, before the actual
inspection on January 12, 2018, interview with the MDRRMO disclosed that they
still have existing inventory of canned goods but unaware that these were already
spoiled. This was a proof that the MDRRMO failed to monitor the status of the
stockpiled goods.
We recommended that the MDRRMO (a) maintain an organized
inventory of DRRM equipment, supplies and materials for the efficient
management of the same; (b) seek the assistance of GSO in the disposal of
defective and unserviceable equipment, if repair of the same is not applicable
anymore; (c) provide satisfactory explanation relative the spoilage of 17
boxes of canned goods; and (d) conduct regular monitoring of the status of
the stockpiled goods particularly the expiry date as well as ensuring that the
goods were placed in appropriate area until its distribution to victims that
will be affected by calamities.
Management comment
In the reply, the MDRRMO commented that the inefficient management of
inventory of disaster equipment, supplies and materials and spoilage of canned goods
for stockpile is due to absence of permanent DRRM office and stockroom wherein
some of the materials were not in place. Hopefully, they can transfer on the newly
constructed DRRM building soon so that they can set standard procedure on the
inventory of DRRM properties and materials as well as DRRM operating procedures.
Moreover, in the noted expiry date of canned goods, according to them, they
are inspecting the same every month. Unfortunately, they did not notice that there
was a leak on the stockroom and some boxes from the bottom get wet. They promised
that to prevent the same to happen again, the stockpiled goods will be placed on the
new DRRM Operation Center because it has a safer stockroom for goods and
materials.
